coredata.dc:title: Advances and prospects of carbon dots for microplastic analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Tammina, Sai Kumar
Khan, Ajahar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Microplastics have become the world's most emerging pollutants today due to the ubiquitous use of plastics in everyday life and their ability to migrate from micro to nanoscale to every corner of the natural world, leading to ecological imbalances and global catastrophes. However, a standardized method for separating and analyzing microplastics from actual food or environmental samples has not been established. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, fast, cost-effective, and accurate method that can accurately measure the degree of contamination of microplastics. As one of these methods, fluorometry has been proposed as a cost-effective method to detect, quantify and differentiate individual plastic particles. Therefore, this review discussed the technique for analyzing microplastics using fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). This review provided an overview of the impact of microplastics and the feasibility of using CDs to detect and analyze microplastics. In particular, this review will discuss novel microplastic analysis methods using CD and future application studies. The method using CDs will overcome the limitations of current microplastic analysis technology and may become a new method for detecting and analyzing microplastics.

head.ce:title: Advances and prospects of carbon dots for microplastic analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microplastics have become the world's most emerging pollutants today due to the ubiquitous use of plastics in everyday life and their ability to migrate from micro to nanoscale to every corner of the natural world, leading to ecological imbalances and global catastrophes. However, a standardized method for separating and analyzing microplastics from actual food or environmental samples has not been established. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, fast, cost-effective, and accurate method that can accurately measure the degree of contamination of microplastics. As one of these methods, fluorometry has been proposed as a cost-effective method to detect, quantify and differentiate individual plastic particles. Therefore, this review discussed the technique for analyzing microplastics using fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). This review provided an overview of the impact of microplastics and the feasibility of using CDs to detect and analyze microplastics. In particular, this review will discuss novel microplastic analysis methods using CD and future application studies. The method using CDs will overcome the limitations of current microplastic analysis technology and may become a new method for detecting and analyzing microplastics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastics are widely used in various industries due to their excellent properties, durability, and cost-effectiveness (Geyer et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2020; Shah et al., 2008). The use of plastics is increasing every year, and it is expected that 33 billion tons of plastic will be produced by 2050 (Sharma et al., 2020). Plastic waste is increasing as plastic consumption increases. Recycling is possible for about 55% of used plastic (Eriksen et al., 2019), and 45% of the remaining plastic waste enters the ecosystem. Environmental pollution is also increasing due to the low biodegradability and disorderly disposal of plastics. The produced plastic wastes are converted into minute plastic fragments or particles through chemical, physical and biological processes with time, resulting in the accumulation of tiny plastic particles/fragments discovered in freshwater (Eerkes-Medrano et al., 2015), air (Prata, 2018), sediments (Yang et al., 2021), foodstuff (Kwon et al., 2020), and soil (Akdogan and Guven, 2019; Koutnik et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020; Thompson et al., 2004).

According to their size, plastics can be classified into macroplastics (>5 mm), microplastics (<5 mm), and nano plastics (<100 nm) (Gigault et al., 2018; Yong et al., 2020). According to the production process, microplastics can be divided into primary and secondary ones. Primary microplastics are small particles intentionally manufactured for industrial use, while secondary microplastics are plastic particles from which plastic waste is physically transformed into small particles in the natural environment (Wu et al., 2019). It is estimated that most microplastics are discharged into the environment, and about 70–80% of secondary microplastics and 15–31% of primary microplastics are accumulating in the environment. Microplastics are increasing rapidly in virtually every habitat on Earth today, including oceans, rivers, groundwater, soil, and even the polar regions of aquatic and terrestrial environments (Fig. 1 ) (Jiang et al., 2019; Panno et al., 2019; Vianello et al., 2013).

Microplastics (plastics <5 mm) are considered a heterogeneous form of synthetic polymers containing particles derived through the decomposition of primary or secondary production (larger plastic materials) (Corami et al., 2020; Prata et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019). The main portion of microplastics originated from laundering clothes, body care, and cosmetic products (35%), followed by urban dust (24%) and tire wear (28%) (Corami et al., 2020). In addition, microplastics can be produced from any plastic source, including polyethylene, polypropylene, polystyrene, polyvinyl chloride, cellulose acetate, polyesters, and nylon (Campanale et al., 2020), which exist in the form of granules, fragments, fibres, and microbeads (Bayo et al., 2020; Bhatt et al., 2021; Hidayaturrahman and Lee, 2019).

The threat of microplastic contamination can be expressed as chemical toxicity and physical damage due to the vast volume of microplastics thrown into water bodies. (Groh et al., 2019). Also, the threat level to humans depends on the size of the plastic particles. Depending on their size, these plastic particles can access organs. For example, lymph nodes can absorb plastic particles smaller than 150 μm, and portal veins can absorb microplastics as small as 110 μm (Lusher, A., Hollman, P., Mendoza-Hill, 2017). Additionally, 7% of nano plastics have the potential to cross epithelial cells and can arrive at most organs, including the spleen and liver (hepatotoxicity), reproductive organs (reproductive toxicity), brain (neurotoxicity) and heart (cardiovascular toxicity), and even it can cross the placental and blood-brain barrier (Lusher et al., 2017). Endocytosis and phagocytosis are the primary pathways to the uptake of plastic particles (Revel et al., 2018).

Moreover, microplastics can absorb persistent organic contaminants, which can be transferred to living tissues (Panno et al., 2019) through direct or indirect ingestion and become carriers of various metals (Zhou et al., 2019). Moreover, their bioaccumulation and invasion of the food chain pose a severe risk to the living world, including humans (Tien et al., 2020; X. X. Zhang et al., 2021). According to literature research, the problem of microplastic pollution is found not only in densely populated areas (Jahan et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019) but also in remote areas such as forests, mountains, oceans, glaciers, and Antarctica (Ambrosini et al., 2019; Cabrera et al., 2020; Cincinelli et al., 2017; Van Cauwenberghe et al., 2013; Y. Y. Zhang et al., 2021).

Because microplastics are a global concern, practical and reliable technologies are needed to limit the problems associated with microplastic contamination. Several notable efforts have been made in this regard, including reusing, reducing, and recycling plastic materials and replacing plastics with bio-based feedstocks. Several resolutions and guidelines have also been implemented to tackle microplastic pollution. The European Union has implemented a comprehensive plastics policy to regulate microplastic emissions into water bodies and mitigate microplastic pollution in aquatic ecosystems (Kentin and Kaarto, 2018). A significant challenge is identifying the full spectrum of microplastic contaminants (e.g., shape, type, surface chemistry, and morphology) under different natural environments.

In addition, tremendous effort is required to identify, determine, and characterize microplastics in different environments to generate reliable data based on the toxicity and quantity of this contaminant so that effective mitigation strategies can be developed (Yusuf et al., 2022; H. H. Zhao et al., 2022; K. K. Zhao et al., 2022). To date, numerous detection and removal strategies, including analytical and engineering techniques (Miri et al., 2022), have been established as potential pathways to moderate the adverse effects of microplastics. Although standard techniques used in water treatment plants can potentially remove large numbers of microplastics (Xu et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019), there is still potential for further proficiency and extenuation of associated defects. Numerous technological advancements have been made in the past five years, and ongoing research into new materials and methods is advancing the state of the sector. Nanotechnology can solve many social problems (Hairom et al., 2021). Dealing with pollutants through nanotechnology has been developed as a viable option to address the existing challenges of sustaining ecosystems from pollution (Jain et al., 2021; Scaria et al., 2020). The proficiency and value of material in its intended application are strongly influenced by its size and dimensions. The structure of macro-sized materials has been mainly altered due to top-down or bottom-up techniques for synthesizing nanomaterials, resulting in significant changes in their properties (Abid et al., 2022). Nanomaterials have opened up the potential for developing high-performance materials that are both technologically and sparingly advantageous since they are used in water and wastewater treatment (Manikandan et al., 2021). Nano-reinforced technology holds strong prospects for significant improvements in characterization, identification, monitoring, detection limits and removal efficiencies in microplastic management and treatment. Carefully planned and intended, advanced nanoscale materials deliver the desired qualities for the effective processing of microplastics (Goh et al., 2022). Singh et al. utilized the magnetic biochar synthesized by the hydrothermal method for capturing nano plastics with different functionalities and sizes (Singh et al., 2021). Wang et al. utilized the modified magnetic biochar (Mg/Zn/Fe-biochar) for microplastics' adsorption and thermal degradation (J. J. Wang et al., 2021). Chen et al. effectively utilized metal-organic framework-based foams to remove microplastics (Chen et al., 2020). Also, identification and quantification are significant issues related to developing microplastic sensors or detectors. To this end, various analytical instruments such as FT-IR (Renner et al., 2017, 2019), Raman (Lv et al., 2020), pyrolysis GC-MS (Gomiero et al., 2021; Kirstein et al., 2021) were used. However, device miniaturization is necessary because most technologies have limitations in real-world applications (Zhang et al., 2022). Luminescence sensors have been developed with a commitment to their promising selectivity and sensitivity to analytes. In a recent report, Nile red was used to track microplastics (Elert et al., 2017), and a few other dyes were used to detect and quantify microplastics (Karakolis et al., 2019). For example, a 4-dimethylamino-4′-nitrostilbene (DANS) staining has been used to develop various detection and assay methods based on fluorescence microscopy (Sancataldo et al., 2021). For studies on microplastics in fluorescent mode, the background and false positive signals are significant barriers. The turn-on or off luminescence approach may reduce the background interferences and reflect actual circumstances when working with real samples (Zhang et al., 2022). Due to their exceptional adsorption and optical properties, carbon nanomaterials are proven as a rising star in the nanomaterials field. Even a recent report proved the affinity of carbon nanomaterial towards microplastics (Tang et al., 2021). Therefore, in the current situation, it is necessary to develop an approach based on fluorescent carbon materials.

The size and dimensions of a functional material are essential properties that influence the proficiency and effectiveness of the material in the proposed application. CDs play an essential role in various technological areas and can potentially solve environmental problems (Hairom et al., 2021). CD-related decontamination strategies have emerged as a sustainable option for addressing the abovementioned challenges (Ðorđević et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2015). As currently used in fields such as biological imaging (Zhu et al., 2013), photovoltaics (Yuan et al., 2018), solar cells (Liu et al., 2019), drug delivery (Feng et al., 2016), the CD is the high-performance material of the future. CDs synthesized by top-down or bottom-up methods have radically modified the structure of macro-scale materials, resulting in significant changes in physicochemical properties (Farshbaf et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019). A surprise innovation in CD could result in substantial advancements in monitoring, identification, detection, and characterization, as well as effective removal, using approaches for microplastic detection and epidemic containment. Intentionally designed hybrid nanomaterials provide desired properties for efficient microplastic removal.

This review aims to provide prospects for the potential of newly developed CDs to improve and enhance the performance of current treatment or removal techniques used to detect microplastics. The merits and disadvantages of the current and potential identification techniques employed in the analysis of microplastics are examined. Therefore, the performance of CDs and their integration with microplastic identification strategies have been proposed. We then concluded the discussion by summarizing the significant obstacles to implementing CD-assisted microplastic removal technology and presenting prospects for future research. The current evaluation is anticipated to inspire additional research-based suggestions for scientists to create and practice a reliable, highly effective CD-based microplastic monitoring approach beneficial to environmental preservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of microplastics on the ecosystem
ce:section.ce:para: Microplastics' strong potential to release chemicals and monomers and their capacity to absorb other environmental contaminants are significant causes for concern. Additionally, they are pretty resilient in ecosystems (Wright and Kelly, 2017). Although the actual toxicity of microplastics to the human body has not yet been identified, traces have. Been found in human faeces (Schwabl et al., 2019) and blood (Leslie et al., 2022). Microplastics can trigger a local immunological response when they enter our bodies by ingestion or breathing them in. (Wright and Kelly, 2017). Chemicals adsorbed on microplastic surfaces can cause severe effects in both abiotic and biotic environments that can be swallowed by contact or inhalation (Bradney et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019). In addition, as a result of desorption, hazardous substances are released, increasing their likelihood of toxicity and accumulating in the food chain.

After being dispersed in an ecosystem, microplastics interact with other species to influence their behaviour. For example, contact between microplastics and pelagic and benthic microbial communities can change the properties of contaminants over time and classify how and why cells append themselves to the microplastics. After ingestion, it is passed down the food chain and automatically affects consumers (Rogers et al., 2020).

Microplastics are composed of various additives and polymers and have various characteristics such as shape, size, and chemical composition. So far, it is unknown whether the cause of microplastic toxicity is plastic particles or chemicals. Several reports show limited effects of micro or nano plastics on species, including yeast, bacteria, nematodes, protozoa, and Phyto-organisms. However, several other groups reported various toxic consequences of microplastics and other related pollutants in vertebrates (Miranda et al., 2019), invertebrates (Foley et al., 2018), mammals (Yong et al., 2020), and sea birds (Duis and Coors, 2016). Although no deaths were recorded after ingestion of microplastics, microplastic intake in invertebrates showed various effects, such as decreased somatic cell growth rate, decreased fertility, oxidative damage, and changes in metamorphosis (Foley et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2017; Leung and Chan, 2018; Ziajahromi et al., 2018). Recently, the distribution and accumulation of microplastics in specific tissues (liver, kidney, intestine) and the tissue risks resulting from accumulation have been investigated. A report described nephron and gastrointestinal toxicity (Revel et al., 2018). Also, particle size affected the distribution and accumulation of particles. As they continued investigating, they confirmed the effects of microplastic particles on mice's lipid metabolism, energy metabolism, and oxidation stress (Rist et al., 2018). A significant cause of microplastic toxicity via oxidative stress is inflammation.

The size of the microplastics also affects the generation of reactive oxygen species, and many nanoplastic particles promote the generation of more reactive oxygen species (Eerkes-Medrano et al., 2019). In another investigation, in vitro studies have shown that 10 μm microplastic particles use oxidative stress on human epithelial cells and brain cells (Schirinzi et al., 2017). The large surface area of microplastics can activate the gut's immune system to cause inflammation and absorb plastic particles (Rist et al., 2018). Animal studies have reported that colonic mucosal tissue absorbs 0.2% of polylactide-co-glycolide (3 μm) particles (Revel et al., 2018). Nanoplastics within 1 mg/L concentration simultaneously induced 24% genotoxicity to aquatic organisms, and genotoxic damage was observed as the concentration increased and the particle size decreased (H. H. Sun et al., 2021; M. M. Sun et al., 2021; T. T. Sun et al., 2021).

Veneman et al. (2017) reported that polystyrene microplastics induced toxic effects in zebrafish and explained it systematically. Likewise, Lei et al. (2018) reported intestinal alterations with rising glutathione s-transferase (GST) activity and oxidative damage in zebrafish after exposure to polyethylene, polypropylene, polyvinyl chloride, and polyamide microplastics. The omnipresent existence of microplastics in the environment and daily life products make human contact with microplastics unavoidable. The critical entry of microplastics into humans is through inhalation, skin exposure, and ingestion.

Prata et al. (2019) state that size, surface area, shape, and morphological aspects affect diffusion methods across biological walls, adsorption to epithelial cells, and aggregation into deep tissues. The favourable conditions for microplastic accumulation showed a wide range of risks to health via in vivo and in vitro conditions (Deng et al., 2017; Schirinzi et al., 2017). Contaminated food is a significant additional route for microplastics to enter the body, negatively affecting the digestive system, epithelial permeability, inflammatory states, and gut microbial composition. (Campanale et al., 2020). Several additives are used in the production of plastics; for instance, bisphenol A (BPA) is used in producing polypropylene, PVC, and polyethylene. These additives can cause cancer, genital abnormalities, and toxicity to living tissues (Campanale et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021).

Due to the high diffusion capacity of microplastics in the air, they can enter the respiratory tract through ingestion and inhalation, which is a hazardous note to be considered. After inhalation, there are apparent changes in metabolism by lesions between the bronchial and bronchial tissues and lesions in the interalveolar region (Prata, 2018). When microplastics enter the bloodstream, they can move to peripheral tissues, which can have many toxic effects, including inflammatory processes, oxidation stresses, metabolic disorders, and nerve system toxicity. Jani et al., (1990) and Eyles et al. (2001) reported that microplastics have accumulated in the liver and spleen of the mouse after the intake and inhalation of microplastics (Eyles et al., 2001; Jani et al., 1990). Oral administration of microplastics made of polystyrene enhances its accumulation in the intestine, gut, and kidney. This step affects redox balance, changes in homeostasis energy, and neurotoxicity (Deng et al., 2017).

Palaniappan et al. investigated the inflammatory response of mammalian cells in the presence of microplastics. Polystyrene and polyethylene microplastics were added to mammalian cells, and their effects were observed. They found that cell viability decreased with increasing microplastic content. Furthermore, these microplastics have been shown to affect the transcriptional expression of inflammatory cytokines and antioxidant enzymes and cell survival in epithelial cells of L929 murine fibroblasts and Madin-Darby kidney cell lines (Palaniappan et al., 2022). Monitoring the flow of microplastics in several abiotic and biotic environmental conditions is essential to confine their pollution status and risk after exposure. Monitoring microplastic pollution requires simple and comparative strategies. However, tracing the microplastics with various shapes, sizes, and compositions using a single analysis method is complex and problematic.

Most microplastics are made up of polymeric materials, but they vary in size, shape, structure, colour, and composition. Therefore, various techniques have been used to detect it. The microplastics seen above have been classified according to their physical (shape, size, colour) and chemical composition (Shim et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2019). Physical and chemical properties were processed through microscopy, FTIR, and Raman spectrophotometers. Liquid chromatography-tandem spectroscopy (LC-MS/MS) and mass spectrometry combined with thermochemical techniques support microplastic mass concentration determination (Akoueson et al., 2021; Funck et al., 2020). In addition, laser direct infrared spectroscopy has demonstrated the possibility of detecting microplastics in the atmosphere (Li et al., 2021; Samandra et al., 2022; Scircle et al., 2020a). Several other techniques for microplastic detection in this way have been identified and discussed in the latest literature.

Visual identification is the primary method for identifying microplastics with the naked eye or under a microscope to separate them from the environment. Through this method, the characteristics of microplastics in the air, such as colour, shape, size, and number, can be identified (Bergmann et al., 2019; Hidalgo-Ruz et al., 2012; X. Wang et al., 2021). The composition difference of microplastics can also be determined by observing the surface morphology under a microscope to determine whether it is a natural or synthetic material (Prata et al., 2020). Visual identification is a cost-effective and less time-consuming method and is primarily suitable for calculating inferred microplastics (Song et al., 2015). However, size identification is limited to around 50 μM (Dris et al., 2017), and the error rate is also very high. For instance, fibres and materials with vivid colours are more noticeable than those with various shapes and translucent materials. (Chen et al., 2020). Although microplastics can be identified with the naked eye, there is a high chance that the smaller microplastics will be missed due to limited vision. Therefore, methodologies to identify smaller-sized microplastics should be developed, and microscopy is not recommended for microplastics smaller than 500 μm (Allen et al., 2019).

Several microscopy methods have been used to identify atmospheric microplastics, such as stereomicroscope, fluorescence, polarization, and binocular microscopy (Abbasi et al., 2017). Fluorescence microscopy with UV filters (different wavelengths) can identify extracted microplastic particles based on their fingerprints (Dehghani et al., 2017; Mbachu et al., 2020). Binocular microscopy distinguishes between glassy materials and microplastics based on strong reflection, refraction, and transparency (Eriksen et al., 2013). Another point is that paper and wood exhibit the same properties under polarized light and fluorescence microscopy (Abbasi et al., 2017). Various investigations have identified transparent plastic particles in approximately 20–70% of the total population found through a stereomicroscope and reported the resulting characterization using different techniques (Eriksen et al., 2013; Song et al., 2015). Synthetic and natural fibres commonly found and highly abundant in sediments, biota, and water samples are difficult to identify through a stereomicroscope (Lusher et al., 2013).

SEM is used to record the surface morphology of particles through the interaction between the particle's surface and a high-energy electron beam from the SEM's electron gun within the 2 nm resolution range. It can also be attached to energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy to generate the chemical composition of a particle. Cai et al. (2017) observed the formation of pits and grooves due to physical forces such as friction and collision by sensing microplastics in the atmosphere and examining their surface morphology. Also, the presence of fragments was confirmed due to the tearing of the plastic material. Although many researchers have used SEM to identify microplastic particles, SEM has several drawbacks: high equipment cost and difficult detection when the number of samples in a single window is large.

A fluorescence microscope collects the fluorescence emission from the samples when excited with a specific wavelength. And usually, the analyzed samples are composed of a fluorophore that responds to the excitation energy. The analyzed sample types are mostly living organisms such as tissues, bacteria, and cells. These fluorophore compounds already exist in the sample to be processed or provided from the outside. This fluorophore gets hit by the radiation, excites, and then emits fluorescent radiation. This technique can analyze microplastics (Prudent and Raoult, 2019) and bio-related samples (tissues, bacteria, cells, etc.).

For white transparent plastics, fluorescence microscopy is the best technique to identify plastics based on their intrinsic ability to emit fluorescence (KIMO Sweeden, 2007). This approach reduces the failure of microplastic detection by detecting smaller-sized microplastics combined with imaging methods.

However, one of the significant limitations of microplastic detection through this technique is that the chemical additives used in its preparation may miscommunicate when microplastics are read under a fluorescence microscope (Lee et al., 2020; Piruska et al., 2005). Therefore, it is essential to remove impurities through efficient pretreatment while recording the fluorescence measurement values with a fluorescence microscope (Elert et al., 2017).

Acid treatment, enzymatic digestion, or oxidizing agents can remove surface impurities, but pretreatment steps can interfere with microplastic chemical additives, misleading actual emissions. A new approach has been developed to label microplastics in Nile red, especially when analyzing biocomponents (tissues or organisms) and observing emission changes before and after labelling microplastics (Cole et al., 2013). The updated fluorescent dye adsorption method will be presented in future prospectives.

Usually, most atmospheric samples collected from the environment are composed of many types of contaminants, including wood, leaf tissues, paper, and leaf tissues with similar physical characteristics to microplastics (Dekiff et al., 2014). Chemical composition identification is essential to avoid misinterpretation. FTIR is one of the techniques for recognizing microplastics with minute chemical composition differences by having a fingerprint region and comparing the results with a built-in library for this region (Hendrickson et al., 2018). Another FTIR technique is to visualize microplastics in micro-FTIR. However, since microplastics can only be detected with a size less than 10 μm, significant errors can occur if the sample size is less than 5 μm (Liu et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2021). However, due to its spectral library and non-destructive technique, FTIR is the most widely used technique for detecting the chemical properties of microplastics (Chen et al., 2020).

In Raman spectroscopy, the interaction of the laser light with the molecular vibrations of a sample leads to the absorption or emission of energy by displaying a particular vibration mode. The denotation of energy changes is usually represented by Raman shift (cm−1), and the fingerprint spectrum helps identify the sample's vibrations (Yang et al., 2021). In addition, visual observation is possible in micro Raman, like micro-FTIR, whose tiniest size is 1 μm (Wright et al., 2019). Additionally, this technique can be destructive when the sample is irradiated with a high rate of the laser, but no damage was observed below 25% intensity (Allen et al., 2019). Therefore, it is recommended to take precautions during laser intensification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermochemical method coupled with gas chromatography-mass spectrometry
ce:section.ce:para: This technique has been used to identify microplastics and nano plastics from different sources, such as water and sand (Davranche et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021; Treilles et al., 2020). This technique identified the samples based on the quantity from 100 to 1000 μg, while in the case of nanoplastics, the practical limit of detection is 0.05–1.9 ng mL−1 (Akoueson et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Along with plastic particles, this technique successfully identified the additive components of plastic particles (Hermabessiere et al., 2018). Materić et al. (2020) also identified microplastics and nanoplastics using thermal desorption-proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (TD-PTR-MS) and developed fingerprint algorithms to gain the resemblance between the mass data of a sample and standard material.

Magali Albignac et al. (2022) used the same technique to determine the microplastic content in about 0.7 μm range in organisms with good reliability. The novelty in each polymer mass spectra allowed the development of fingerprinting. Even though this technique is destructive but still being explored due to the advantage of its mass falling under the spectroscopy size (Cai et al., 2017; Materić et al., 2020; Reichel et al., 2020). Also, the best thing about this method is that there is no limit to the plastic particle size. So far, little information has been reported on the environmental behaviour of microplastics and their communication with organisms.

However, this technique is limited because it cannot identify MP's colour, size, and shape. Also, the lack of commercially available isotopic analogue resins makes the transition to a standardized technique extremely time-consuming (Lauschke et al., 2021). This technique requires further development to establish fingerprint areas of microplastics by analyzing and tracking the most commonly available microplastics in mixtures.

ICP-MS is a technique for microplastic detection in water with good detection sensitivity (Peng et al., 2020). Usually, it is operated in a single-event mode, which can provide the necessary information about the chemical composition, size and size distribution, particle density, and mass concentration in water samples (Bolea-Fernandez et al., 2020). Bolea-Fernandez et al. (2020) detected the microplastics through ICP-MS by monitoring the carbon content of microplastics, and the carbon spikes (13C+) appeared in the spectrum related to the number of microplastics existing in the respective sample.

LDIR is a spectroscopic technique with a high-magnification visible camera to visualize microplastics in soil, groundwater, ocean, and biological tissues. This method's lowest detectable size limit is 20 μm (Lee et al., 2013; Li et al., 2021; Samandra et al., 2022; Zheng-gang et al., 2021). According to previous reports, the minimum size limit was 18 μm (Samandra et al., 2022). The LDIR also features an innovative light source, a quantum cascade laser with faster speed and wavelength accuracy than FTIR and Raman spectroscopy (Scircle et al., 2020b). This unique feature combines the LDIR with scanning optics and a point detector to generate an infrared spectrum of microplastic particles per second (Li et al., 2021; Mariano et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical challenges related to the accurate detection of microplastics
ce:section.ce:para: According to the published detection techniques, the analysis of microplastics presents the most significant challenge for analytical scientists because of their heterogeneity, variety of shapes, trim sizes, and visual misidentification caused by linked interfering organic moieties. Sophisticated analytical instrumentation is required to determine the small size of microplastics from environmental samples(Table 1). Natural matters, such as silica, natural debris, and fibres, are frequently mistakenly recognized as microplastics (due to their variable density, shape, size, and type) while employing microscopic techniques. In addition, the uneven morphology of microplastic pollutants creates trouble in their representative sampling. The multifaceted sample matrix involves laborious sample preparation strategies (such as wet peroxidation, sieving, filtration, and density separation before analyses) to isolate the microplastics from associated organic substances.

Furthermore, manually spotting microplastic particles from other interfering matters is a tedious and time-consuming analytical step. The time needed for this approach depends on the elimination efficiency of organic and inorganic matters during the clean-up and separation steps.

The manual spotting may slip the identification of microplastic particles, particularly transparent and small ones that are difficult to distinguish. To overcome these limitations, highly sophisticated analytical techniques to identify and quantify microplastic contaminants are essential to obtain reliable results.

However, issues with detecting microplastic contaminants can be resolved by creating sophisticated analytical procedures and coupling them with appropriate already-existing conventional instruments. One of the challenges in the microplastic investigation that remains to be addressed is the restriction of detectable size. The existing analytical techniques only possess a minimum detectable size limit of up to a few micrometres. Although size-dependent harmful biological effects of microplastics differ from toxicological endpoints, tiny plastic particles are more toxic than larger plastic particles (Lee et al., 2013).
The presence, toxicity, behaviour, and distribution of microplastics in the environment must all be determined urgently in addition to their precise identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prospects for the development of new methods for the identification of microplastics
ce:section.ce:para: Many research teams have currently made progress in creating or identifying microplastic detection. However, most of them have been successful with a combination of other identification techniques and qualitative verification. Additionally, their method has been successful in locating microplastics as thin as tens of microns, although smaller plastic particles smaller than 10 μm are challenging to find using a microscope alone. However, to produce reliable and precise results from the examination of very minute plastic particles, several analysis iterations are necessary (Mariano et al., 2021). These smaller plastic particles are also of tremendous research interest because of their highest toxicity and biological relevance. Analytical strategies require sophisticated tools and more time to achieve desired results. Instead, developing a cost-effective, simple, reproducible, and rapid on-site laboratory identification method can save resources and time. Roch and Brinker (2017) investigated a novel approach for detecting microplastics in the gastrointestinal tract (GITs) of fish. This method involves an accelerated digestion step using a combination of nitric acid and sodium hydroxide to digest all organic material, followed by a separation step through sodium iodide. All studied polymer types were recovered using this method with slight variations in size, colour, and weight. It also verified the 95% recovery of microplastics. Fig. 2 A and B shows that all GITs were fully digested by the technique described, leaving insignificant tissue particles. The fluorescent traces were visible under UV irradiation (Fig. 2C) with recovery rates of 95–100% (Fig. 2D).

Guo et al. (2022) investigated a self-powered multifunctional hydrogel actuator based on an integrative structure and function design, which can logically identify and eliminate heavy metal-adsorbed microplastic compound pollutants (mMCPs) from aquatic systems. Fig. 2E illustrates the synthesis strategies adopted to construct all-in-one hydrogel materials which comprise covalently bonded multifunctional adsorbents (polydopamine and polyethyleneimine copolymers), photothermal converters (graphene oxide nanosheets) and actuating hydrogel matrix [(poly (N-isopropyl acrylamide)]. This specific copolymer arrangement with profuse functional groups provided strong interfacial interactions with metal ions existing on the surface of microplastics, stimulating fluorescent responsiveness and mMCPs absorption characteristics. Fig. 2F shows simultaneous sensing, actuation, and adsorption performed in one single hydrogel material system without requiring additional working modules to have multiple functions.

One recent study described a staining method in which the dye stained the plastic surface by heat reaction and, as a result, emission intensity changes were seen when exposed to UV light. This technique aided in the quantification of microplastics (Maes et al., 2017). Also, Sturm et al. (2021) investigated the effect of solvent polarity on the fluorescence spectra of Nile red (NR) and their derivatives; they discovered that the NR sorbs more firmly onto the non-polar plastic in a polar solvent, and results in emission wavelength shift to more extended wavelength region, and the highest emission intensity was observed in water. They ascribed that the strong adsorption or incorporation of NR onto the surface and polymer matrix was the reason for the interaction between the duos. The substantial hindrance for the molecular movements inside the polymer matrix further led to the modulation of emission peaks. Even the change in pH also influenced the sorption kinetics, where NR and its derivative showed good sorption at a pH of 2.5 in water (Sturm et al., 2021). Gomiero et al. (2021) utilized the 4-dimethylamino-40-nitrostillbene (DANS) fluorescent dye to detect microplastics in aquatic environments. They used a solvatochromic effect. That is, the polarity of the microplastic affects the dye fluorescence emission. Here, DNAS dyes absorbed by different polymers showed a positive shift in the steady-state fluorescence emission spectrum depending on the polarity of the microplastics, and it was reported that the sizes of the different microplastic particles (PP, PET, HDPE, PS) were less than 5 mm (Sancataldo et al., 2021). Another investigation utilized a 1-pyrene butyric acid N-hydroxysuccinimidyl ester (PBN) fluorophore to detect microplastic particles in personal care products. Solutions of personal care products were treated with stains, and their size and shape were observed under a fluorescence microscope. The observed microplastics were further characterized by ATR-FTIR for structural elucidation and confirmed the presence of polyethylene, polypropylene, polycarbonate, ethylene vinyl acetate, polystyrene, and polyvinyl acetate particles (Lee et al., 2021). Similarly, another research group investigated to find the Nile Red emission response for synthetic fibres (polyethylene and polypropylene) and natural fibres (cotton and linen) at various incubation times and found that the response of the fluorescent probe depends on the polarity of the analyte (Galvão et al., 2022).

Also, Costa et al. reported polystyrene microplastic detection through pyrene fluorescent probes on the surface of salt (Costa et al., 2021). NaCl contact with the probe quenched Cl-induced fluorescence in polar media. After adding the polystyrene particles, the emission intensity was enhanced. They concluded that the probe migrated into the non-polar environment of the polystyrene particles, with an approximate detection limit of 0.2 μg/g. Supporting a Nile red dyeing approach for microplastics, Maes et al. revealed that dye molecules interact with microplastic surfaces via van der Waals and with additional dipole interactions with polar polymers (Maes et al., 2017) (Table 1).

A typical CD structure consists of two parts. One is a spherical core structure composed of several graphene pieces stacked regularly or randomly, and the other is functional groups distributed on the CD surface (Shiral Fernando et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015). The quantum confinement effect (QCE) is a key feature of CDs, which occurs when the size of the CDs is smaller than the exciton's bore radius. QCE means that as the size of the nanomaterial decreases, the continuous energy level of the conduction and valence bands changes to the discrete energy level, and the band gap increases, causing an energy gap transition in the ultraviolet and visible regions, thereby improving the fluorescence quantum yield (Yan et al., 2019). For several types of CDs with large π-conjugated structures with few surface functional groups, fluorescence arises from conjugated π-electron systems, measured as carbon core state luminescent centres (Kwon et al., 2014; Sk et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2019). Another mechanism affecting the fluorescence of CDs is the abundance of surface defect states. Surface defects represent a spherical shell different from the carbon core. This region consists of various chemical moieties formed from hybrid sp2 and sp3 carbon structures or other surface functional moieties (Shen et al., 2011, 2012). Also, they can be easily synthesized via a simple one-step synthetic route without harmful chemicals. CDs have been synthesized through various source materials, such as chemicals and naturally available sources (Cui et al., 2021; Torres Landa et al., 2022). Due to the versatility of CD properties related to synthetic conditions, CDs have been used to detect different types of analytes with high selectivity and sensitivity. For instance, nitrogen-doped oxidized CDs (NOCDs) were synthesized through the hydrothermal method for sensing the 4-nitrophenol, where the NOCDs were reduced in the presence of 4-NP and this conversion influenced the optical properties of NOCDs by quenching the emission with increased concentration of 4-NP and it could detect the 4-NP up to a detection limit of 2 μM (Bogireddy et al., 2020). Not only water pollutants, heavy metals (Tammina et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2022), but volatile organic pollutants like alcohol (Kalytchuk et al., 2021), methanol (Latha et al., 2020) and acetone (Bogireddy et al., 2021) were also detected through carbon dots. A few other recent works on pollutants/analytes sensing using CDs from several resources are summarized in Table 2
.

The versatility of CD sensing potential based on the different synthesis conditions makes them the most valuable source to develop sensors for emerging pollutants like smaller-sized plastic particles or nano plastic particles in water, which can help overcome the issue of detecting plastic particles less than 10 μm in size. Another advantage is the wettability of CDs, which determines the hydrophobicity, and the ability to tune the hydrophilicity by controlling the functionalization and surface modification of CDs with different functional materials (Ng et al., 2021; Tammina et al., 2019, 2020), which can be achieved by modifying the surface of CDs. The surface modification also helps to alter the interaction with organic or inorganic molecules adsorbed on the microplastic surface, which opens up tremendous possibilities for identifying microplastics in real-world samples. In addition, the functionalization of CDs with surfactants can generate self-assembled molecular clusters, which may be suitable for sensing microplastics in water and oil phases.

In general, surfactants have an alkyl chain (hydrophobic group) having 8 to 22 carbon atoms and a hydrophilic functional group. The hydrophobic group exhibits no affinity for water, but the hydrophilic molecule of the surfactant has an affinity for water (Nakama, 2017). Additionally, CDs functionalized with suitable surfactants can interact with polymer and lipid surfaces. The management of these interactions may entail van der Waals forces, dispersive forces, hydrophilic effects, dipole/acid-base, and electrostatic attractions. It has been reported that surfactants can impart more complex interactions with polymers at the droplet interface and in bulk solutions.

For example, polyethylene glycol (PEG) and poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) exhibit binding interactions with ionic surfactants in solutions and interfaces. These polymer-surfactant complexes can significantly alter the forces between the oil-in-water (O/W) nanoemulsion droplets. (Helgeson, 2016). Similar interactions can lead to changes in the fluorescence emission of CDs functionalized with surfactants, either through emission quenching or enhancement or through changes in solution colour based on the CD's basic luminescent properties.

In this situation, the setting of Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) can improve the emission intensity of the overall system. FRET is a distance-dependent physical process by which energy is transferred non-radiatively from an excited molecular fluorophore (the donor) to another fluorophore (the acceptor) using intermolecular long-range dipole-dipole coupling. It can also accurately determine molecular proximity at distances of 10–100 Å and is proficient when the donor and acceptor are located within the Förster radius (Sekar and Periasamy, 2003). Here, the donor is a CD, and the acceptor is a fluorophore, so the presence of a surfactant between the CD-fluorophores can cause the FRET phenomenon. For example, Yu et al. developed a three-component system with the help of CDs, rhodamine B and Ag3PO4 to manipulate the sub-bandgap frequencies through FRET supported upconversion process. The CDs were bonded to rhodamine B through π-π interaction with a high molar extension coefficient. The spectral energy overlaps between the rhodamine B emissions and the absorption of the CD results in FRET from the rhodamine B excitation to the CD, followed by an enhancement of the upconverted luminescence intensity of the CD. Due to the proper energy gap (Eg) and band structure, this upconverted energy was further accepted by a semiconductor in the three-component system, i.e., Ag3PO4, resulting in outstanding charge separation, followed by generation of photocurrent from Ag3PO4 (Yu et al., 2016). Deng et al. also reported a FRET-based fluorescence sensor through rhodamine 6G functionalized CDs to detect aqueous Fe3+ ions. The spirolactam structure of rhodamine 6G was not fluorescent. No visible absorption, but in the presence of Fe3+ ions, rhodamine 6G in its ring-opened amide form exhibits strong yellow emission at 550 nm, with an absorbance maximum at 530 nm. The functionalized CDs emission (530 nm) overlapped with the ring-opened rhodamine 6G absorbance maxima, so emphasized as a suitable energy donor and acceptor in the presence of Fe3+ (Deng et al., 2016). Sometimes the absorbed energy of an analyte overlaps the emission energy of the CDs without the involvement of other organics or moieties, resulting in FRET. Liang et al. (2016) reported the FRET reaction between trinitrophenol (TNP) and chitosan-derived CDs. The fluorescence emission of CDs is quenched in the presence of TNPs if the absorption energy of TNPs overlaps the emission energy of CDs. It explains this quenching behaviour supporting the FRET phenomenon (Liang et al., 2016). Even the combination of different quantum dots can possess the FRET phenomenon. The spectral overlap between the GQDs and CQDs makes them a FRET pair, where GQDs emission and CQDs absorption were in overlap with each other, with GQDs being the energy donor and CQDs being the energy acceptor. And this FRET process was quenched in the presence of Hg2+ and As5+ metals (Chini et al., 2019). In another study, the FRET process was considered advantageous for detecting Pb2+, where FRETs from CDs to Au nanorods via cysteamine. Still, after adding Pb2+, the interaction between Pb2+ and carbon dots stopped the FRET process, resulting in fluorescence restoration for CDs, establishing a linear trend between the concentration of Pb2+ and CD emission intensity (Liu et al., 2019). Similarly, the analyte presence caused fluorescence recovery by breaking the FRET phenomenon, herein the FCDs (CDs + anti-Helicobacter pyroli) were coupled to the graphene oxide, and the FRET happened between FCDs (donor) and graphene oxide (GO) (acceptor), resulted in fluorescence quenching of functionalized-CDs (FCDs). However, in the presence of a target analyte, i.e. Helicobacter pyroli, the energy transfer was restricted to GO due to the effective binding of FCDs to the targeted analyte and leads to fluorescence restoration (Chattopadhyay et al., 2022) (Fig. 3 ).

Jana et al. established the FRET phenomenon in the presence of ionic surfactants between CDs-RhB and reported increased energy transfer within the CD and RhB systems under surfactant confinement (Jana et al., 2020). This study showed that fluorophores could be used to establish the FRET phenomenon to enhance the emission properties of systems designed to detect plastic particles. CDs also offer their ability as biosensors by triggering changes in fluorescence signals in response to specific environmental conditions or interactions with molecules (Fig. 3). For applications of CDs, particularly sensing applications, some recent studies have suggested using CDs to detect markers, including pH change, water activity, or gas generation. The property of changing the fluorescence of CDs when interacting with specific compounds can be used to develop fluorescent nanosensors for pH changes (Liu et al., 2017). In addition, CD's low in vivo and in vitro toxicity, good biodegradability, biocompatibility, and excellent antifungal, antibacterial, and antioxidant efficacy make them the most suitable safe nano-sized materials used as active materials for direct contact with biological systems without further contamination (Ezati et al., 2022; Tammina et al., 2020).

Previously, Zhang et al. (2022) used boron-doped carbon nanoparticles (BCNPs) for detecting plastic particles (Fig. 4 ). The nanoparticles were non-fluorescent but “turned on” fluorescence after adsorption or entered into the polymer matrix under thermal conditions. In this process, different polymers exhibited different coloured emissions under UV irradiation, such as polypropylene (PP, yellow emission), poly (vinyl chloride) (PVC, magenta emission), polystyrene (PS, pink emission), and polyethene (PE, yellow emission).

This approach allowed them to distinguish polymer types based on colour and intensity upon excitation at 365 nm. The medium of the plastic material affected the emission properties of the boron-doped carbon nanoparticles. The size limit of microplastics observed in this method was ≤5 mm. In addition, they performed a semi-quantitative analysis on PP microplastics in the presence of interfering substances such as chitosan and lignin. They established a linear function by calculating the fluorescence area through Image J software.

Using the fluorescence phenomenon, we proposed a strategy to detect plastic particles, and Fig. 5 shows the proposed mechanism for detecting microplastic particles. Here, when the carbon dot composite (surfactant and fluorophore) is added to the plastic particle solution, the surfactant can interact with the plastic particle, possibly hydrophobically. The lipophilicity of CDs due to surfactants can lead to interactions with plastic particles via van der Waals interactions or additional dipole interactions. If the interaction between the analyte and the surfactant-containing CDs is in the linear range, a change in the luminescent behaviour of the CDs under illumination can be seen.

There is a significant knowledge gap in identifying common toxicity mechanisms, material characterization, and responsibility for the contamination. Previous studies have shown that workers in plastics-related industries have suffered several types of cancer due to contact with high levels of airborne microplastics over the years (Wang et al., 2020). Degradation or decomposition of microplastic contaminants from environmental microbes (such as fungi and bacteria) remains challenging for scientists. There is currently no natural way to reduce the number of microplastics in the food chain.

At a basic level, social awareness can play an essential role in the ‘science-society-policy interface’ that communicates the adverse effects of all forms of microplastic pollution. In addition, numerous effective techniques have been evaluated for the determination and quantification of microplastics in various environmental samples. Determination of microplastics generally requires in-depth knowledge to quantify possible emission strategies of microplastics, which would have to distinguish between different analytical techniques and different polymeric or laboratory process-based investigations. The combination of appropriate analytical techniques plays a vital role in identifying different types of microplastics in diverse and complex environments.

In most cases, these techniques have generated reliable data to identify large microplastics on the order of millimetres. However, as microplastics decrease in size, identifying them is difficult and time-consuming. It is essential to analyze these contaminants at the sub-micron level, considering the threats to ecology and human health. Therefore, to accurately detect microplastic contamination, there is an urgent need to improve the existing technology and develop a new advanced methodology to reduce the effort and identification time.

CDs continue to attract much research interest since they are versatile in various wastewater treatment fields. (Bhati et al., 2019; Rani et al., 2020). The new interest is also enthusiastic about evaluating the feasibility of detecting microplastics in underwater systems. From a macroscopic point of view, CDs have the potential to replace currently used materials and can be utilized in microplastic identification strategies. The effectiveness of CDs has been demonstrated to outperform conventional materials used to remove various organic contaminants and heavy metals from water sources (Long et al., 2021; Rani et al., 2020).

As a result of drastically lowering experimental costs and facilitating final analysis, CDs also demonstrated promise for the identification of microplastics. Nanoscale materials have been investigated to exhibit better recyclability than bulk materials. Thus, the life cycle of the material can be extended. Additionally, utilizing CDs with low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, chemical inertness, highly controllable photoluminescence, and photoinduced electron transport characteristics was associated with the simplicity of retrieval following processing. Despite its advantages, establishing a CD-enabled strategy for microplastic identification is only the beginning. Another issue is the stability of nanoscale materials and the behaviour of microplastics, which can be significantly altered by the actual water source environment, such as pH and salinity.

For example, several types of interfering substances can significantly reduce the effectiveness of microplastic detection using materials. Small suspended organic matter can also occupy the working material's functional sites, impeding microplastic detection. Therefore, further studies designed with more diverse and complex water-based systems are needed to more accurately evaluate the microplastic detection efficiency and better understand the use of CDs for various microplastics. It is also worth mentioning that microplastics act as carriers for many organic and inorganic substances in natural water systems.

Therefore, microplastics combined with organic compounds show significantly different characteristics from existing microplastics, so it is essential to evaluate the identification ability of microplastics (Mei et al., 2020). In addition, it is necessary to realistically design the experimental environment by simulating the actual water system and analyzing various particle sizes, types, and microplastic concentrations so that the advantages of new materials are not overestimated. Interactions between microplastics and other interfering contaminants that produce undesirable effects must also be considered.

Moreover, as the industry still uses nanomaterials, it isn't easy to succeed with conventional approaches and higher-level materials. More planned analyzes through computational and experimental methods are needed to provide more scientific evidence that will not only bridge the knowledge gap but also expand the confidence of relevant industry stakeholders. It is also essential to validate that the benefits of CDs compensate for their impact when dealing with microplastic contaminants.

It is essential to verify sustainability and safety before full implementation to increase acceptance by the industry and consumers. The advantages of CDs-derived materials should not come at the cost of nanomaterial leaching, which ultimately leads to secondary contamination. Lack of efficiency in recovering and separating spent nanomaterials puts them at risk of exposure to exposed environments for direct use as photocatalysts and sensing devices. In this sense, well-planned and ongoing research is needed to ensure that the developed CDs, detection strategies, and types of microplastics are well-matched.

Combining the newly developed CDs with existing microplastic removal technology can spur the practical use of these new nanomaterials. We proposed using CDs as promising fluorescent tags for determining various environmental microplastic contaminants. The response of CDs differs from that of currently used nanomaterials in the following ways:
i)
Given the current data, sensing devices based on appropriate nanomaterials can be used to identify hotspots and quantify the microplastic load in the environment.
ii)
Because functionalization is responsible for the interaction, functionalized CDs can be constructed by engineering the core and surface of CDs with specific organic compounds or analyte-sensing elements.
iii)
The fluorescence of CDs can be controlled by changing the size, core, and surface functionalization.
iv)
CDs could potentially be cost-effective, target-specific, and high-precision fluorescence candidates for detecting microplastics in environmental and food samples.
v)
Physicochemical identification and quantification of microplastics require multiple analytical instruments to classify concentrations, chemical compositions, shapes, and types. Therefore, coherent sensors combined with conventional analytical technology will help detect even the slightest concentrations of microplastics/nanoplastics in various forms, which are easy to sample preparation, collect, analyze, and remove.
vi)
Validation of assumptions and hypotheses about the feasibility of the CD application process requires coordinated investigations enhanced with computational tools.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Issues with CDs for microplastic detection
ce:section.ce:para: Following the strategy proposed above, CDs can detect microplastics based on the laboratory-scale synthesis conditions and the type of microplastic particles. However, in the case of actual environment samples, CDs may have limitations on sample formats. CDs can work for each plastic particle depending on the synthesis conditions if the sample is in the aqueous phase. However, if the actual sample is from sediment, the interaction and access of the CDs to the plastic particles may be minimized due to unavoidable barriers, mainly mud, when approaching the plastic particles.

And another problem is the organic materials attached to the surface of the plastic particles. As the plastic particles pass through different environments, chemical additives may be adsorbed on the surface of the plastic particles. While detecting identical plastics, there is room for misunderstanding. This issue applies to all detection technologies, not just CDs. Therefore, sample preparation is required for accurate sample analysis to avoid collisions.

Establishing a linear trend for plastic particle concentration regardless of sample environmental pH is another minor problem for detection, as the emission intensities of most carbon dots are different concerning protonation and deprotonation.

Developing highly selective sensors for each type of polymer is another challenge. However, even if it takes time to establish a standard approach for high-sensitivity sensors of microplastics through CD, recent literature on CD detection for contaminants reports high selectivity and sensitivity. Nonetheless, the reported literature on CDs and their inherent properties can overcome all these challenges by taking the investigation to the next level by tuning the approach in all aspects.

Currently, microplastics pollute ecosystems worldwide and pose a significant threat to the lives of marine, freshwater, and terrestrial ecosystems, including biota. Accordingly, efforts are being made to reduce the number of microplastics present in the environment. For this, it is essential to establish an accurate and convenient method for detecting microplastics above all else. Various qualitative and quantitative analytical methods, such as microscopy, thermal analysis, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, and TED-GC-MS coupled methods, are used to identify microplastic contamination. However, using these techniques individually can sometimes improperly identify microplastic particles due to different interfering organic materials. The combination of analytical techniques helps to detect microplastics in diverse and complex environmental matrices. Reliable results are obtained when detecting microplastic particles as small as a millimetre. However, the smaller the size of microplastics, the more cumbersome and time-consuming to identify. Reliable and standardized analytical methods are essential to identify and quantify microplastics, which are still in their infancy. Therefore, extensive investigations still need to be carried out to improve more efficient microplastic detection techniques that could help better manage microplastic-based contaminants.

Recently emerged sensing devices based on suitable nanomaterials (>10 nm) exhibit sensitive specificity and ease of detection even in the nanometer range. This may lead to more acceptable and convenient development of microplastic detection in the future. In addition, eco-friendly nanomaterials such as biomass-derived CDs are inexpensive and easily accessible, enabling precise control with existing devices. While addressing the shortcomings of conventionally used microplastic detection methods, CD-based microplastic analysis methods may face many other challenges.

The heterogeneity of microplastics, varying densities, different shapes, trim sizes, and visual misconceptions make it challenging to identify these contaminants. In addition, the lack of practical experience handling CD-derived functional materials in this field remains a big problem. Gathering more convincing evidence, especially from field analysis, is essential at this stage, as the provision of scientific data has implications for broadening the acceptance of CD-derived active substances in microplastic detection methods. The joint effort of analytical scientists and industry is critical to achieving overall results by adopting CD as a safe future microplastic analytical tool.

This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant (2022H1D3A2A01056792), funded by the Korean government through the Brain Pool Program and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B02001422).

Sai Kumar Tammina: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing- original draft, Writing-review, and editing. Ajahar Khan: Writing-original draft, Writing-review. Jong-Whan Rhim: Supervision, Project Administration, Funding Acquisition, Writing-review, and editing.
